title: Vercel API access tokens should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Vercel API access token detected in assignment. This pattern identifies Vercel deployment and API tokens when assigned to variables, configuration settings, or environment variables. Vercel API tokens provide comprehensive access to deployment management, serverless functions, edge networks, analytics, and project administration within the Vercel platform. These tokens enable critical operations including deploying applications and static sites to Vercel's global edge network, managing serverless function configurations and environment variables, accessing detailed deployment logs, analytics, and performance metrics, configuring custom domains, SSL certificates, and DNS settings, managing team members, permissions, and organizational billing settings, accessing Git integration settings and automated deployment workflows, configuring edge functions and middleware for traffic routing, managing databases, storage integrations, and third-party service connections, accessing real-time analytics data including visitor metrics and function execution logs, and controlling preview deployments and branch-based deployment strategies. When a Vercel API token is compromised, attackers can gain unauthorized control over your entire deployment infrastructure to deploy malicious code, backdoors, or cryptocurrency miners to your production applications serving real users, access and exfiltrate source code, environment variables, and sensitive configuration data from your projects, modify DNS settings and SSL certificates to redirect traffic to malicious servers for phishing or data harvesting, access detailed analytics revealing user behavior, traffic patterns, and business intelligence data, steal environment variables that may contain database credentials, API keys, and other sensitive service credentials, deploy unauthorized applications under your account that could be used for illegal activities, access billing information and potentially abuse premium features leading to unexpected charges, manipulate edge function configurations to intercept user traffic and steal sensitive data, access team management functions to add unauthorized users or modify permissions, delete or corrupt existing deployments causing service outages and data loss, configure malicious webhooks to intercept deployment notifications and sensitive project information, access Git integration settings to steal repository access tokens and source code, and use your Vercel infrastructure reputation to conduct distributed attacks or host malicious content. The security and business impact can be catastrophic including complete compromise of production applications serving customers with potential for data breaches and regulatory fines, massive reputational damage from malicious content served through your domains affecting customer trust and brand integrity, financial losses from premium feature abuse, incident response costs, and potential legal liabilities from compromised customer data, operational disruption from service outages, corrupted deployments, and DNS hijacking affecting business continuity, exposure of sensitive source code and intellectual property to competitors through unauthorized repository access, compliance violations under various security frameworks and data protection regulations requiring extensive remediation efforts, loss of customer confidence from security incidents affecting business growth and retention, supply chain security risks if compromised infrastructure is used to attack downstream customers or partners, regulatory investigation and penalties if compromised systems are used for illegal activities, and long-term business relationship damage from security breaches affecting enterprise customer contracts. Take immediate protective action by logging into your Vercel dashboard at vercel.com, navigating to Account Settings > Tokens section, identifying and immediately revoking all suspicious or compromised API tokens, generating new API tokens with minimal necessary scopes and permissions for specific use cases, updating all CI/CD pipelines, deployment scripts, applications, and automated systems with the new secure tokens, conducting a comprehensive review of deployment logs and audit trails to identify all unauthorized activities during the exposure period, checking all projects for unauthorized deployments, modified configurations, or injected malicious code, reviewing team member lists and removing any unauthorized accounts that may have been added, examining DNS and domain settings for malicious modifications or unauthorized redirections, validating SSL certificate configurations and checking for any unauthorized certificate installations, analyzing edge function and middleware code for malicious modifications or backdoors, reviewing environment variables across all projects for signs of data exfiltration or unauthorized access, checking billing statements for unexpected usage charges or premium feature abuse, verifying Git integration settings and rotating any connected repository access tokens that may have been compromised, implementing IP address restrictions and access controls for API token usage where supported, migrating token storage to secure environment variables, key management systems, or encrypted configuration stores, establishing comprehensive monitoring and alerting for unusual deployment activities and API usage patterns, implementing regular token rotation schedules as part of proactive security maintenance, providing security training for development and operations teams on proper Vercel credential management, documenting the security incident thoroughly for compliance auditing and future incident response improvement, and engaging cybersecurity professionals to conduct a full security assessment and implement additional protective measures to prevent similar token-based attacks.

Vercel API token detected. Vercel API tokens are alphanumeric strings that provide programmatic access to Vercel's deployment platform, serverless functions, edge network, and project management capabilities. These tokens enable comprehensive control over your Vercel infrastructure including deploying applications to global edge locations, managing serverless function configurations and runtime environments, accessing deployment analytics, performance metrics, and user behavior data, configuring domain management, SSL certificates, and custom DNS settings, managing environment variables that may contain sensitive database and service credentials, controlling team access, billing settings, and organizational permissions, accessing Git integration workflows and automated deployment pipelines, configuring edge functions and middleware for request processing, managing preview deployments and branch-specific deployment strategies, accessing real-time logs, error tracking, and debugging information, and integrating with third-party services and databases through API connections. When these tokens are exposed in source code repositories, configuration files, or documentation, attackers can compromise your entire deployment infrastructure to deploy malicious applications, backdoors, or cryptocurrency mining operations to production environments serving real users, access and steal sensitive environment variables containing database passwords, API keys, and third-party service credentials, manipulate DNS configurations and SSL certificates to redirect user traffic to phishing sites or malicious servers, exfiltrate source code, deployment artifacts, and proprietary business logic from your applications, access detailed user analytics including visitor data, geographic information, and usage patterns for competitive intelligence, deploy unauthorized preview environments to test attack vectors or host illegal content using your infrastructure, modify edge function code to intercept user requests and steal sensitive data like authentication cookies or personal information, access team management functions to add malicious users or escalate permissions for persistent access, delete or corrupt production deployments causing service outages and revenue loss, configure malicious webhooks to receive notifications containing sensitive project and deployment information, and access billing systems to understand your infrastructure usage and potentially abuse premium features. The resulting security and business consequences can be severe including complete production environment compromise leading to data breaches and regulatory penalties, significant financial losses from infrastructure abuse, incident response costs, and potential legal liabilities, massive reputational damage from malicious content served through your domains affecting customer trust and business relationships, operational disruption from service outages, corrupted deployments, and DNS manipulation affecting business continuity and revenue, exposure of intellectual property and competitive business information through unauthorized source code and analytics access, compliance violations under security frameworks and data protection regulations requiring extensive remediation and reporting, loss of customer confidence from security incidents affecting long-term business growth and market position, supply chain security risks if your compromised infrastructure is used to attack customers or integration partners, regulatory investigation and potential criminal liability if compromised systems facilitate illegal activities, and permanent damage to brand reputation requiring extensive public relations and customer rebuilding efforts. Respond immediately by accessing your Vercel account dashboard, navigating to the API tokens management section, identifying and revoking all potentially compromised tokens without delay, generating new secure API tokens with minimal required permissions and well-defined scopes, updating all deployment pipelines, CI/CD systems, scripts, and applications with the new credentials using secure distribution methods, conducting a thorough audit of all deployment logs, access records, and account activity during the token exposure timeframe, scanning all deployed applications and serverless functions for unauthorized code, backdoors, or malicious modifications, reviewing team membership and access permissions to ensure no unauthorized accounts were created, checking DNS settings, SSL certificates, and domain configurations for malicious changes or redirections, examining environment variables across all projects for signs of credential theft or unauthorized modifications, analyzing deployment analytics and user traffic logs for suspicious patterns or data exfiltration activities, validating the integrity of all production applications and rolling back any unauthorized deployments immediately, reviewing billing statements for unexpected usage spikes or premium feature abuse indicating malicious activity, implementing enhanced security controls including IP address restrictions, access monitoring, and deployment approval workflows, migrating credential storage to secure cloud key management services, encrypted environment variables, or dedicated secret management platforms, establishing comprehensive logging and real-time alerting for all deployment activities and API token usage, implementing automated token rotation policies and regular security audits as part of ongoing operational security, providing targeted security awareness training for all development and operations team members, documenting the complete incident timeline and response actions for regulatory compliance and future security improvement, and consulting with cybersecurity experts to perform a comprehensive security assessment and implement advanced threat detection capabilities.

Vercel API authorization header detected. This pattern identifies Vercel API authentication credentials passed through HTTP headers including custom authorization headers and Vercel-specific authentication tokens. Authentication headers are critical security mechanisms that provide access control to Vercel's deployment APIs, serverless function management, analytics endpoints, and project administration interfaces. These headers typically contain sensitive authentication material such as personal access tokens providing full account access to deployment management and project controls, team API tokens enabling collaborative deployment workflows and shared project management, organization-level tokens granting administrative access to billing, user management, and security settings, deployment-specific tokens allowing automated CI/CD pipeline integration with version control systems, function invocation tokens enabling serverless function execution and environment variable access, analytics tokens providing access to user behavior data, performance metrics, and business intelligence, webhook authentication tokens ensuring secure communication between Vercel and external services, and temporary session tokens maintaining authenticated access across multiple API operations and deployment activities. When these authentication headers are exposed in source code, configuration files, server logs, or documentation, attackers can intercept and misuse them to perform unauthorized deployment operations and infrastructure manipulation against your Vercel projects, impersonate legitimate users and development teams to deploy malicious code or corrupted applications to production environments, access sensitive environment variables and configuration data that may contain database credentials and third-party service API keys, manipulate DNS settings, SSL certificates, and domain configurations to redirect user traffic for phishing or data harvesting, access comprehensive analytics data revealing user behavior patterns, geographic distribution, and business performance metrics, deploy unauthorized serverless functions designed to steal user data, inject malicious code, or perform cryptocurrency mining, modify existing applications to include backdoors, tracking code, or malicious functionality without detection, access team management and billing functions to add unauthorized users, modify permissions, or abuse premium features, delete or corrupt production deployments causing immediate service outages and potential data loss, configure malicious webhooks to intercept sensitive deployment notifications and project management communications, access Git integration settings and repository credentials for source code theft and intellectual property compromise, and maintain persistent unauthorized access through token manipulation and permission escalation techniques. The security and business impact can be devastating including complete compromise of production infrastructure serving millions of users with potential for massive data breaches, severe financial losses from premium service abuse, incident response costs, regulatory fines, and legal liabilities from customer data exposure, catastrophic reputational damage from malicious content served through trusted domains affecting long-term customer relationships and market position, operational paralysis from service outages, corrupted deployments, and DNS hijacking disrupting critical business operations and revenue streams, exposure of proprietary source code, business logic, and competitive intelligence to malicious actors or competitors, regulatory compliance violations under security frameworks and privacy laws requiring extensive investigation, remediation, and public disclosure, erosion of customer trust and confidence leading to user churn, contract cancellations, and difficulty acquiring new business, supply chain security compromise if your infrastructure is used as a launching point for attacks against customers, partners, or integrated services, potential criminal liability if compromised systems are used to facilitate illegal activities such as fraud, identity theft, or cyber attacks, and permanent brand damage requiring extensive public relations efforts and customer rebuilding initiatives costing millions in recovery efforts. Take immediate emergency action by accessing your Vercel account dashboard through a secure, verified connection, navigating to account security settings and API token management interfaces, identifying and immediately revoking all potentially compromised authentication tokens and API credentials, generating new secure authentication tokens with minimal required permissions and time-limited expiration policies, updating all client applications, CI/CD pipelines, deployment scripts, and integration systems with new credentials using secure distribution channels, conducting an exhaustive audit of all API access logs, deployment activities, and account operations during the credential exposure window, performing comprehensive security scans of all deployed applications, serverless functions, and static assets for malicious code injection or unauthorized modifications, reviewing and validating all team member accounts, access permissions, and recently granted privileges to identify unauthorized additions, checking DNS configurations, SSL certificate installations, and domain settings for malicious redirections or unauthorized changes, examining environment variables, secrets, and configuration data across all projects for signs of credential harvesting or data exfiltration, analyzing user traffic patterns, analytics data, and performance metrics for indicators of malicious activity or data theft, verifying the integrity and authenticity of all production deployments and immediately rolling back any suspicious or unauthorized changes, reviewing billing statements and usage reports for unexpected charges, premium feature abuse, or resource consumption spikes, implementing enhanced security controls including multi-factor authentication, IP-based access restrictions, and deployment approval workflows, migrating all credential storage to enterprise-grade secret management systems with encryption, audit trails, and automated rotation capabilities, establishing real-time security monitoring, automated threat detection, and incident response procedures for all deployment and API activities, implementing mandatory security training programs for all development, operations, and administrative team members, documenting the complete incident response timeline, affected systems, and remediation actions for regulatory compliance and security auditing purposes, engaging certified cybersecurity professionals to conduct comprehensive penetration testing and security architecture reviews, and implementing advanced security measures including zero-trust networking, behavioral analytics, and continuous compliance monitoring to prevent future credential-based attacks.
